info.topictitle: B2.2.2 Physical prototypes for design testing

markdown:
## Physical Prototypes


<definition term="Physical Prototype">A tangible, real-world model of a product, built using physical materials.</definition>

<callout type="note">They can be **aesthetic** (how it looks) or **functional** (how it works), depending on the design stage and purpose.</callout>

### Why Build Physical Prototypes?


1. **Helps Others Understand the Design **
2. **Tests Scale and Space**
3. **Reveals Practical Issues **(identify flaws in comfort, interaction etc.)
4. **Supports Iteration**

<callout type="note">The **fidelity** of your prototype (how realistic or detailed it is) should match its purpose. For more on fidelity levels, refer back to **Section 2.1.13**.</callout>

### Key Considerations in Prototyping


#### 1 - Scale


  1. The **size** of the prototype relative to the **final product**.
  2. **Considerations**:
      1. **Full-scale prototypes**: Provide accurate insights into **ergonomics** and **functionality**.
    2. **Scaled-down models**: Useful for **large products** (e.g., buildings) to test **overall design** and **spatial relationships**.

![Scaled-down models are commonly used in architecture to explore form, layout, and spatial relationships before full-scale construction begins](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/197c09e507fd51bd5d010d835087f2c194640f2d-1600x1063.png)

<callout type="tip">Always consider the context in which the prototype will be used. A full-scale model may be necessary for testing ergonomics, while a scaled-down version might suffice for visualising overall form.</callout>

#### 2 - Shape


  1. The **geometric form** of the prototype.
  2. **Considerations**:
      1. Ensure the shape accurately represents the **intended design**.
    2. Test how the shape affects **functionality**, **aesthetics**, and **user interaction**.

<callout type="note">Shape is not just about aesthetics, it can significantly impact a product's functionality and user experience.</callout>

![Aesthetic models, even at low fidelity, help test form, grip, and comfort, ideal for gathering client feedback on shape and ergonomics early in the design process (johngarzota)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/d1856484aeac2a634d559451e77e61bb13509679-924x519.png)

#### 3 - Space


  1. The **relationship** between the prototype and its **environment**.
  2. **Considerations**:
      1. Test how the prototype fits within its intended **space** (e.g., a chair in a room).
    2. Evaluate how it interacts with **other objects** or **systems**.

![Testing a prototype of a chair in its intended environment](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e86b43e37925482b9a7899b06c77dca22dfa1a7b-736x736.png)

<callout type="analogy">Think of a prototype as a dress rehearsal for a play. It allows designers to test and refine their ideas before the final performance, ensuring everything works as intended.</callout>
